posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Mental exercise threads usually get a lot of user participation. So, in order to get the new site going, I thought it would be a good idea to do one.

If you could only drink a dozen wines for the rest of your life, what would they be?

The Rules:
I don’t mean only a dozen bottles. I mean a dozen wines.

  • Presume you can buy/drink as many bottles as you want, in any size you want, as long as you can reasonably afford them and they are more-or-less reasonably available to you. (That means that, unless money is no object for you and you have a long-standing allocation, DRC Romanée-Conti would likely be a bad choice.)
  • Presume also that you can buy vintages as long as they keep producing them, and that if the producer’s around today they’ll be around for you to get all the wine you need. (That probably strikes out wines that are no longer produced, even if they are/were beloved, like Halcón Elevación but YMMV. EDIT TO CLARIFY: If you think you have enough in your cellar for the rest of your life and/or you can backfill enough, then by all means include them. You can find a further clarification of this rule here: If you could only drink a dozen different wines for the rest of your life - #96 by Guillermo_M )
  • Presume the truth (unless you’re over a certain age already), that your palate will change and you have to be ready for that.
  • Presume that any wine you pick represents a level/category for the producer and you can keep buying the successor wine if the one you like is no longer made. (For example, if we had run this exercise a few years ago and someone had listed Louis Roederer Brut Premier, their choice would presumed to change to Louis Roederer Collection 2XX. Same thing if the producer loses the vineyard like the Domaine Barge Côte-Rôtie Côte Brune which would now be the Les Côtes)
  • Presume that a producer that changes names because of inheritance or marriage is still the same producer.

Some notes:
With those constraints in mind, it was very difficult for me to come up with the list below, which leaves out wines I love.

(In fact, because I’m posting this to get the ball rolling I might change a few. Don’t strictly hold me to it.)

What I learned doing it:
It became clear to me that, for my expectations and predicted use, big wines had to take a back seat. Thinking of categories helped so much that it drove many choices.

Here’s my list:

  1. Raúl Pérez Atalier a cruz das ánimas Rias Baixas (a zippy white that has good depth)

  2. Selbach-Oster Zeltinger Sonnenuhr Ur Alte Reben Riesling feinherb MCM (a white for all seasons)

  3. Krug Grande Cuvée (a nuanced age-worthy sparkler like no other)

  4. Bedrock Ode to Lulu (a great Mourvedre-based rosé)

  5. G. D. Vajra Claré JC Langhe Nebbiolo (a crushable chillable red)

  6. Kelley Fox Maresh Vineyard Royal Ann Block (an age-worthy light-bodied mostly destemmed Pinot Noir)

  7. Goodfellow Heritage No. XX (an age-worthy medium-full bodied whole cluster Pinot Noir - this is a bit of cheating because it unties me from site but direct your complaints to Marcus)

  8. G.D. Vajra Bricco delle Viole (an age-worthy Barolo from a cool-enough site that it’ll be good for a while)

  9. Eric Bordelet Poiré Granit (A fruity, low-alcohol sparkler with nuance – more cheating because it’s cider, so sue me)

  10. Max Ferd. Richter Veldenzer Elisenberg Kabinett (Might change to Steinmetz or Loersch, might do Spatelese instead but I need the general category)

  11. Louis Roederer Vintage (more affordable than the Krug - might decide to do a different one but a sparkler it is)

Ok, so you caught me. I couldn’t do it. Too much stuff for the last spot. I’m still thinking.

Options right now for the last spot:

  • An early-drinking fun Sangiovese with nuance (Stereophonic Heart Container? Cafaggio CCR?)

  • A wine to take on hearty food and Thanksgiving (Carlisle Papera? Côte-Rôtie but which? Garnacha?)

Things I like that are still definitely out (it wasn’t easy): Bordeaux in general and its grapes from anywhere. The Loire and its grapes. Cru Beaujolais. Still Chardonnays from anywhere except for Champagne. Assyrtiko. Dry Riesling. Bierzo. Alto Piemonte.

Anyway, if you could only drink a dozen wines for the rest of your life, what would they be?

So here it goes, in no particular order:

  2. Roilette Cuvee Tardive
  3. Baudry Les Grezeaux
  4. Sky Zinfandel
  5. Bedrock Heritage
  6. Gonon St Joseph
  7. Levet Les Journaries
  8. Chateau Sociando Mallet
  9. Chateau Leoville Barton
  10. Vieux Chateau Certan
  11. Chateau Trotanoy
  12. Vatan. Ha, I need at least one white.

if I were in dream mode, I’d add Rayas, Haut Brion and Cristal.

JoelD:

Was there any clarification on whether you could keep buying back vintages if available vs having to keep buying the newer vintages? Obviously things change with winemakers etc.

You can buy old vintages, yes. But the market is the real market. You can’t presume you’d buy 50 cases of back vintages of a wine unless such supply actually exists or can be reasonably expected to cumulatively exist as time goes by.

The caveat here is the topic doesn’t seem to contemplate only buying old Bordeaux. It suggests continuing to buy current releases, which I don’t do. No Bordeaux would suck.

Guillermo_M:

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked:
We’re aligned for what it’s worth. To me variety is such an important part of wine drinking that I’d probably cut way way back if limited to 12 wines and likely end up drinking wine once every 1-2 months. Which would be a plus for my liver and wallet. And the function of the wines I drink would change - they would no longer be wines that I open for the taste, but rather keys into memories of good times eating and drinking with friends and family. 6-12 bottles a year unlocks a good amount of buying power, so with that here are 12 wines and an example of the memories I’d like to revisit:

Prevost La Closerie → in honor of my mother-in-law and the last restaurant meal I had with her before she passed

Selosse Initiale → sitting on the rococo reception room at Le Clarence in Paris, early in my wine journey, being poured this, my first-ever Selosse, as a comped (!) aperitif; this is forever luxe, calme et volupte in my mind

Taittinger Comtes de Champagne → evening picnic at a film festival held in a park, with the setting sun on the nape of my neck

Dom Perignon (old) → holed up with my spouse in our living room during the early pandemic, guzzling uni and watching the Stones play live on TV

Ganevat Vignes de Mon Pere → Daniel Rose working the room on a Monday night, drawing, flamboyantly, on our menu at Le Coucou in NY

PYCM Corton-Charlemagne → one of the first ever wine dinners we ever hosted at our old place, the chatter and clink of forks on plates

Jamet Cote Rotie → cunningly deploying the 1999 to seduce my oldest friend into a lifetime of wine collecting

Noel Verset Cornas → an imaginary seascape in an imaginary land, which I’d like to visit again one day

Musar → the amazing pairing that is goat belly and Musar; the best thing I ever got from Chicago

Lynch Bages → the polite contempt we were shown by the staff at l’Auberge du Pont de Collonges, which was in its own twisted way a highlight of our honeymoon

Dominus → buttonholing Christian Moueix at a wine event, demanding to know why he got rid of Magdelaine, then shamelessly wangling an invitation to Dominus

Yquem → early days dating my spouse and finding in Yquem the only common ground there was to be found wine-wise; we have consumed Yquem in so many restaurants in SF, built our marriage on Yquem, and finally outgrown it, but it remains a bridge to our past

posts.name: Guillermo M e n a
posts.cooked:
Gerhard_P:

not the wines one would LIKE TO DRINK for the rest of your life (no matter price and availability), but wines one could actually drink (buy, afford)

That’s right.

My inspiration for this thread was the success of a real life story. Through mutual friends, I was once close to the head bartender at what was then (and maybe still is) widely regarded as the best high-end cocktail bar in this city. You needed reservations to even get into his bar, and there was a time limit and you then had to leave. Drinks were, and probably still are, out of this world. For example, I once had a drink there where part of the experience was they would light a pine cone on fire next to the glass so you would get the piney smoke in your nose as you drank.

So a friend of mine asked this bartender where he went to get drinks when he wanted a drink. The genius in that question is that it wasn’t asking for recommendations. It was a personal question about himself. He wasn’t on the hook for recommending something if we ended up not liking it.

And his answer was a bar much less expensive than his (not cheap but high QPR), very fun, relatively easier to get into (though it gets packed), and mostly geared to a different sort of cocktail than he served at his bar. That one, not his high-end special occasion bar you have to make reservations for 2+ months in advance, is, for me, the essential bar in this city (especially for cocktails of its sort). Don’t ask me its name. And the bartender who gave me the answer no longer lives here and has his own bar elsewhere.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I thought about this for a few days before posting, because it’s actually quite a difficult one. I know what my favourite wines are now, but like most people, my taste changes on a regular basis, so predicting how it might change in the future is tough. In no particular order, starting with whites:

  1. Domaine Ogereau - Savennières. I’ve had a couple of 2020s recently which have really impressed me - not a new producer, but Emmanuel Ogereau is making wines very much along the same lines as the new Anjou crowd - fresh, crisp and taut, but with a touch of honey and white peach on the finish.

  2. Château de Plaisance - Anjou/Savennières - made in the same style as the Ogereaus, I’m willing to bet that Vanessa Cherruau’s wines will get better and better.

  3. Frères Couillaud (Ragotière, Clos du Petit Château) - Muscadet, Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc wines. This will seem an odd choice but I would need wines for daily drinking rather than just expensive ones. I’m a great fan of these, which I can pick up for 4 euros a bottle and which are very much my weekday stalwarts.

  4. Domaine de la Pépière - Muscadet. An obvious choice - consistent quality at great prices which is unlikely to dip in the future.

  5. Domaine Bernard Baudry - Chinon - mostly for the reds, but I like the Domaine white (more than the Croix Boissée)…

  6. Charles Joguet - Chinon - no reason to think I’ll suddenly go off these or that the quality will drop.

  7. Yannick Amirault - Bourgueil/ St.Nicolas de Bourgueil - Benoit Amirault has raised the level higher in recent vintages and I’m confident this will continue in the future.

  8. Sociando-Mallet - even bad vintages are consistently good.

  9. Léoville Barton - It’s hard to imagine a future without LB.

  10. Gruaud Larose - likewise,

  11. Giscours - hard to choose just one Margaux but I’m not totally convinced by Clos du Jaugueyron and BAMA is unlikely to be around for much longer.

  12. Grand Puy Lacoste - consistent and good value.

posts.name: P a u l Christensen
posts.cooked:
Well I am of limited means relative to many here it seems (and of very high consumption), so I’m not going over US$100/bottle at current prices and trying to average under US$50 (which tends not to leave room for some of the fancier French stuff). Several others were excluded because of limited availability to me, even in the wine hub that is Hong Kong.

If I only had to drink one it would be:

  1. Chateau Musar red (surprised to see that making so few lists)

then a couple of champagnes; in my price range it really means growers, and their range tends to vary, so I’ll just choose the grower generically
2. Tristan Hyest
3. Dehours

then some still (great value) rosés for those hot summer days
4. Domaine de la Mordorée Tavel Cuvée de la Reine des Bois
5. Chateau Musar Jeune Rosé

then white Bordeaux, which is for me one of the most versatile wines
6. Château Carbonnieux Blanc

a variety of other whites (which we drink far more than reds)
7. Oxer Bastegieta Bizkaiko Txakolina Marko
8. Domaine de la Mordorée Lirac Blanc Cuvée de la Reine des Bois
9. Sigalas Assyrtiko Santorini
10. Domaine Cauhapé Jurançon Sec La Canopée

and I guess a couple more reds:
11. San Filippo Brunello di Montalcino Le Lucere
12. La Rioja Alta Rioja Gran Reserva 904

Not that hard of an exercise when I don’t have nearly the amount of experience and budget some of you guys have!

  1. Pierre Péters “Cuvée de Réserve” Brut Blanc de Blancs
  2. Marc Hébrart Special Club
  3. Krug Grande Cuvée
  4. Domaine Tempier Rosé
  5. Domaine Jean Foillard Morgon Cote du Py
  6. Ridge Monte Bello (on futures pricing only)
  7. Ch. Doisy Védrines (sentimental choice)
  8. Ch. Rieussec
  9. Louis Michel Chablis (any)
  10. Ch. Ducru Beaucaillou
  11. Ch. Pichon Comtesse
  12. Taittinger Comtes de Champagne

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
It is an interesting exercise, but I was definitely interested to see your choices, and other’s choices as well.

I think I also fall into the category of knowing what I like and just narrowing it a bit but since I am not actually limited to just twelve wines this thread is fueling up my enthusiasm for what to explore this fall.

I’m staying within what I typically can afford and buy, but including some wines I don’t buy much of due to price. But if I only could buy twelve, I would be able to spend more on them.

Doyard BdB Clos de l’Abbaye
Larmandier Bernier Longitude
Lassaigne Le Cotet
Hexamer Meddersheimer Altenberg Kabinett
Alzinger Riesling (federspiel only)
Vatan Sancerre
Pepiere Clos de Briords Muscadet
Bourdignon Anjou Blanc
Walter Scott Ex-Novo Chardonnay
Thierry Germain Franc de Pied
Clos des Lambrays
Chandon des Briailles Ille des Vergelesses

Also, I’m really touched that my wines are on some of these lists. That’s a huge honor given the vast number of options to choose from.

And if anyone is wondering why there aren’t more Pinot Noirs on my list, it because so much of the magic of Pinot Noir is the diversity of expression it can produce. I really do not have a favorite, so I either could list 12 Pinot Noirs, even that would be a horrible limitation, or I could represent what I actually drink outside of Pinot Noir. I would expect that many Piedmont fans would feel similarly.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Well, I don’t think any of the wines on my list are “big bruisers”, either, they are just all quite serious, intensely flavored wines that are capable of being an event as well as a beverage. Much as I like things like Pepière Clos des Briords or Aviet’s Arbois reds, I’m not going to trade out Coche and Mugneret-Gibourg in their favor. But I’m sure happy to have them in my real cellar.
